content.user_title: Build Reliable Background Jobs with Trigger.dev
content.value_statement: Struggling with flaky background tasks and complex async workflows? Trigger.dev provides TypeScript-first background job orchestration with built-in integrations, automatic retries, and excellent developer experience for AI pipelines and batch processing.
actual_capabilities: Guide setup and configuration of Trigger.dev in Next.js projects
Design AI-powered background tasks with OpenAI integration
Implement scheduled tasks using cron expressions
Create webhook handlers for external service integrations
Architect batch processing jobs with proper concurrency limits
Configure idempotency keys to prevent duplicate side effects
limitations: Requires external Trigger.dev service deployment for job execution
Cannot execute jobs directly - only provides configuration guidance
Does not include runtime job monitoring or debugging tools
Limited to Trigger.dev-specific patterns and may not apply to other queue systems

use_cases.title: AI Pipeline Automation
use_cases.description: Build long-running AI workflows that process data for minutes with automatic retries and error handling
use_cases.target_user: AI application developer building multi-step LLM pipelines

use_cases.title: Scheduled Data Sync
use_cases.description: Create reliable cron jobs that sync data across dozens of external services with built-in integrations
use_cases.target_user: Backend engineer maintaining multi-service data consistency

use_cases.title: Batch Payment Processing
use_cases.description: Process millions of records in batches with proper concurrency limits and queue management
use_cases.target_user: FinTech developer handling high-volume transaction workflows

prompt_templates.title: Basic Task Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me set up Trigger.dev in my Next.js project. I need to create a simple background job that sends emails after user signup.
prompt_templates.scenario: Developer getting started with their first Trigger.dev task

prompt_templates.title: AI Workflow Design
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to build an AI pipeline using Trigger.dev that processes uploaded documents with OpenAI, extracts key information, and stores results in my database. Show me how to structure this with proper error handling.
prompt_templates.scenario: Building a multi-step AI workflow with external integrations

prompt_templates.title: Scheduled Job Configuration
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a scheduled Trigger.dev task that runs every night at midnight to aggregate daily metrics from our analytics API and send a summary report via email.

prompt_templates.title: Batch Processing Architecture
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to process 1 million records through Trigger.dev without overwhelming my database. Design a batch processing solution with proper queue concurrency limits, idempotency keys, and retry strategies.
prompt_templates.scenario: Building high-volume batch jobs with performance considerations

output_examples.input: Help me create a Trigger.dev job that processes user-uploaded CSV files and imports them into our database.
output_examples.output: I'll help you create a robust CSV import job with Trigger.dev. This example includes file upload handling, streaming CSV parsing, batch inserts, and proper error handling for large files.

output_examples.input: Set up a webhook handler in Trigger.dev that responds to Stripe events and updates our user subscriptions.
output_examples.output: I'll configure a Trigger.dev job that uses the built-in Stripe integration to handle webhook events, with idempotency keys to prevent duplicate subscription updates.

best_practices: Always configure explicit timeouts for tasks to prevent indefinite execution without clear error messages
Use plain serializable objects for task payloads to avoid silent failures from non-serializable data
Set queue concurrency limits when processing high-volume jobs to prevent overwhelming downstream services
anti_patterns: Avoid creating giant monolithic tasks that combine multiple responsibilities - break them into smaller, focused jobs instead
Don't ignore built-in integrations like OpenAI and Stripe - they provide automatic retries and error handling
Never skip logging in your tasks - proper logging is essential for debugging background job failures

faq.question: What is Trigger.dev and when should I use it?
faq.answer: Trigger.dev is a TypeScript-first background job platform that bridges the gap between simple queues and complex orchestration. Use it when you need reliable async execution for AI pipelines, integrations, or batch processing with excellent developer experience.

faq.question: How does Trigger.dev differ from BullMQ or RabbitMQ?
faq.answer: Trigger.dev provides higher-level orchestration with built-in integrations, automatic retries, and TypeScript-native development. Unlike traditional queues, it includes webhook handling, scheduled tasks, and AI service integrations out of the box.

faq.question: Can I run Trigger.dev jobs locally during development?
faq.answer: Yes, Trigger.dev supports local development with a dev CLI that runs jobs locally while connecting to the Trigger.dev cloud service for orchestration and webhooks.

faq.question: How do I prevent duplicate task executions?
faq.answer: Use idempotency keys in your task definitions. Trigger.dev automatically deduplicates tasks with the same idempotency key, preventing duplicate side effects from retries.

faq.question: What happens when a task times out?
faq.answer: By default, task timeouts can kill execution without clear errors. Always configure explicit timeouts for your tasks to ensure proper error handling and logging.

faq.question: Can I use Trigger.dev with serverless platforms like Vercel?
faq.answer: Yes, Trigger.dev works well with serverless platforms. It handles long-running tasks outside the serverless function timeout, making it ideal for Vercel deployments.
